content.assessment: This proposal directly engages network capacity levers: raising thresholds and formalizing the existing passive capacity ceiling (F-live-school_network_planning-03, F-live-school_network_planning-04) would mechanically shrink the state-sector selective footprint. However, network resilience is undermined by partial jurisdictional reach: because contraction levers apply only to the state sector, any capacity reduction risks being geographically uneven, concentrated wherever state-sector share is highest, without a parallel mechanism for church/private capacity which is not addressed by this lens's findings but is flagged as a structural risk. The feeder-school dilution problem (F-live-school_network_planning-07) may be partially alleviated if demand pressure is genuinely reduced rather than displaced.
opportunities: Could formalize an already-emerging passive capacity ceiling into an explicit, plannable network policy
strengths: Builds on an already-operating administrative capacity ceiling (F-live-school_network_planning-03), suggesting technical feasibility of extending it
Threshold-based capacity control (F-live-school_network_planning-04) is an existing, tested lever within the current network
threats: Oversubscription ratios of 9-26x at flagship schools (F-live-school_network_planning-03) indicate demand pressure is severe enough that contraction could exacerbate rather than relieve access strain at the highest-demand urban nodes
weaknesses: Jurisdiction covers only state-sector maintainers, leaving a substantial share of network capacity (church/private) outside the contraction mechanism
Threshold increases risk pushing marginal students into vocational tracks involuntarily, altering network flows in ways not modeled here
